pick.rationale: Florida enters as a legitimate national championship contender ranked 4th in both KenPom and Torvik with an elite two-way profile—9th in adjusted offense and 5th in adjusted defense—while Iowa sits 25th-26th in the major metrics, representing a gap of roughly 21 spots and 11 points per 100 possessions. The Gators' suffocating defense (92.2 adjusted defensive efficiency, 5th nationally) should neutralize Iowa's 29th-ranked offense led by All-American guard Bennett Stirtz, while Florida's balanced attack featuring three All-SEC honorees (Thomas Haugh, Rueben Chinyelu, Alex Condon) will exploit an Iowa defense that ranks just 35th in adjusted defensive efficiency. Florida's concerning 91-74 SEC tournament semifinal loss to Vanderbilt ended a 12-game win streak and raises questions about focus, but the reigning national champions' substantial analytical edge, superior defensive prowess, and roster depth make this a manageable 1/9 matchup with Iowa's only realistic upset path requiring a transcendent Stirtz performance combined with another Florida letdown.

First half focus: Mississippi State took an early 10-point lead on the back of extremely efficient shooting, handing Florida its largest home deficit in 38 days to open the contest. The Gators’ backcourt took over, though, leading an 18-0 run over 6 minutes of game clock, turning the game on its head by taking an eight-point lead. Freshman CJ Ingram II ignited the scoring stretch with consecutive 3-pointers. Florida tacked on an 11-2 run to move ahead by a half-high 14 points, ultimately leading by 12 at the break. result.text: Florida could fall to No. 2 seed in NCAA Tournament after blowout loss Skip to main content Home Schedule Football Basketball Baseball Recruiting SEC Sports Odds Newsletter GatorSports.com College Sports Wire USA TODAY Sports How Florida’s blowout loss could affect NCAA Tournament seeding Michael Long Gators Wire Florida basketball saw its 12-game win streak shattered after Saturday's 91-74 rout at the hands of Vanderbilt. The loss shed light on troubling trends that could follow the Gators into March Madness. For the opening minutes, the two teams traded baskets before Vanderbilt seized control with a 13-0 run as the 1st half went on. Questionable shot selection, turnovers and stalled half-court execution from Florida fed the Commodore spurts. Sharp shooting from the outside, paired with efficiency on the offensive end, helped swell the lead over time for Clark Lee's team. The lead quickly ballooned to double-digits and before long the contest seemed out of reach for The Orange and Blue. Despite their season ending on a 12-1 streak, the loss may have postseason implications for the Gators. Florida entered the SEC tournament at 26-6 and was widely projected as a No. 1 seed in the NCAA tournament. However, the lopsided defeat to a team barely cracking the top-25 could dampen those hopes. The loss likely shrinks their margins in predictive systems such as KenPom and NET rankings. Here, efficiency, strength of schedule and performance in marquee matchups are weighted heavily. Florida's point differential also suffers in a 17-point defeat. These indicators are vital in splitting hairs between elite teams. Florida's untimely loss opens the door for other contenders eyeing a No. 1 seed. Duke , Michigan and UConn still have a chance to strengthen their case in the ACC, Big Ten and Big East tournaments, respectively, while Arizona is also firmly in the mix for a top regional seed. Vanderbilt may have exposed a blueprint for how March opponents can neutralize the Gators. By controlling the tempo, limiting second-chance opportunities and pressuring Florida into 14 turnovers, the Commodores were able to pull away. Their efficient outside shooting was also key as they nailed 10 3-pointers. Even with a stumble in the semifinals, the Gators still sport a strong resume that includes elite conference performance and multiple Quadrant 1 victories. If these struggles linger into late March, the Gators' championship defense could be short-lived — an outlook that seemed unlikely just days ago. result.text: Florida could fall to No. 2 seed in NCAA Tournament after blowout loss Skip to main content Home Schedule Football Basketball Baseball Recruiting SEC Sports Odds Newsletter GatorSports.com College Sports Wire USA TODAY Sports How Florida’s blowout loss could affect NCAA Tournament seeding Michael Long Gators Wire Florida basketball saw its 12-game win streak shattered after Saturday's 91-74 rout at the hands of Vanderbilt. The loss shed light on troubling trends that could follow the Gators into March Madness. For the opening minutes, the two teams traded baskets before Vanderbilt seized control with a 13-0 run as the 1st half went on. Questionable shot selection, turnovers and stalled half-court execution from Florida fed the Commodore spurts. Sharp shooting from the outside, paired with efficiency on the offensive end, helped swell the lead over time for Clark Lee's team. The lead quickly ballooned to double-digits and before long the contest seemed out of reach for The Orange and Blue. Despite their season ending on a 12-1 streak, the loss may have postseason implications for the Gators. Florida entered the SEC tournament at 26-6 and was widely projected as a No. 1 seed in the NCAA tournament. However, the lopsided defeat to a team barely cracking the top-25 could dampen those hopes. The loss likely shrinks their margins in predictive systems such as KenPom and NET rankings. Here, efficiency, strength of schedule and performance in marquee matchups are weighted heavily. Florida's point differential also suffers in a 17-point defeat. These indicators are vital in splitting hairs between elite teams. Florida's untimely loss opens the door for other contenders eyeing a No. 1 seed. Duke , Michigan and UConn still have a chance to strengthen their case in the ACC, Big Ten and Big East tournaments, respectively, while Arizona is also firmly in the mix for a top regional seed. Vanderbilt may have exposed a blueprint for how March opponents can neutralize the Gators. By controlling the tempo, limiting second-chance opportunities and pressuring Florida into 14 turnovers, the Commodores were able to pull away. Their efficient outside shooting was also key as they nailed 10 3-pointers. Even with a stumble in the semifinals, the Gators still sport a strong resume that includes elite conference performance and multiple Quadrant 1 victories. If these struggles linger into late March, the Gators' championship defense could be short-lived — an outlook that seemed unlikely just days ago.
